author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
title: Cyprian’s Treatise 'On the Mortality.'

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: But if any wish to be instructed in the opinions of those who have handled the subject, it behoves them to prefer to all commentators the book of Wisdom, where it is read, “He was taken away, that wickedness should not alter his understanding;” because illustrious commentators, even in the times nearest to the apostles, preferred it to themselves, seeing that when they made use of it for a testimony they believed that they were making use of nothing but a divine testimony; and certainly it appears that the most blessed Cyprian, in order to commend the advantage of an earlier death, contended that those who end this life, wherein sin is possible, are taken away from the risks of sins.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: In the same treatise, among other things, he says, “Why, when you are about to be with Christ, and are secure of the divine promise, do you not embrace being called to Christ, and rejoice that you are free from the devil?”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: And in another place he says, “Boys escape the peril of their unstable age.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: And again, in another place, he says, “Why do we not hasten and run, that we may see our country, that we may hail our relatives?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: A great number of those who are dear to us are expecting us there,—a dense and abundant crowd of parents, brethren, sons, are longing for us; already secure of their own safety, but still anxious about our salvation.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: By these and such like sentiments, that teacher sufficiently and plainly testifies, in the clearest light of the catholic faith, that perils of sin and trials are to be feared even until the putting off of this body, but that afterwards no one shall suffer any such things.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: And even if he did not testify thus, when could any manner of Christian be in doubt on this matter?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: How, then, should it not have been of advantage to a man who has lapsed, and who finishes his life wretchedly in that same state of lapse, and passes into the punishment due to such as he,—how, I say, should it not have been of the greatest and highest advantage to such an one to be snatched by death from this sphere of temptations before his fall?
